prompt: A bat and ball cost $1.10 in total. The bat costs $1.00 more than the ball. How much does the ball cost? Explain your reasoning step by step.
If all A are B, and all B are C, can we conclude that all C are A? Justify your answer with an example.
A doctor gives you 3 pills and tells you to take one every half hour. How long will the pills last?
You have 8 balls, one of which is slightly heavier. Using a balance scale, what is the minimum number of weighings to guarantee finding the heavier ball?
Analyze this argument: 'Sales have increased every year we've run TV ads. Therefore, TV ads cause sales increases.' What is the logical flaw?
A company's revenue grew 10% in year 1, declined 10% in year 2. Is the company back to its original revenue? Show your calculation.
Three logicians walk into a bar. The bartender asks 'Does everyone want a drink?' The first says 'I don't know.' The second says 'I don't know.' The third says 'Yes.' Explain.
Evaluate: 'We should not invest in renewable energy because it's too expensive.' Identify the assumptions and analyze this claim.
A train leaves Chicago at 60mph heading to New York (800 miles). Another leaves New York at 80mph heading to Chicago at the same time. How far from Chicago do they meet?
If P implies Q, and Q implies R, and not-R is true, what can we conclude about P? Prove it using formal logic.
A company reports 90% customer satisfaction. Is this sufficient evidence that the product is good? What other information would you need?
Analyze the Monty Hall problem: you pick door 1, host opens door 3 (no car), should you switch to door 2? Justify rigorously.
Three switches control three light bulbs in another room. You can toggle switches before entering the room once. How do you identify which switch controls which bulb?
Evaluate this syllogism: All mammals are warm-blooded. Whales are warm-blooded. Therefore, whales are mammals. Valid or invalid?
A store sells items at 20% above cost. If an item costs $80, and they offer a 10% discount, what is the profit margin?
Explain why 0.999... (repeating) equals exactly 1 using at least two different mathematical arguments.
Analyze: 'Countries with more doctors have higher rates of disease. Therefore, doctors cause disease.' What statistical phenomenon explains this?
A researcher finds that eating breakfast correlates with higher test scores. Design a study to determine if this is causal.
Solve: You have a 3-liter and a 5-liter jug. How do you measure exactly 4 liters using only these jugs and a water tap?
Analyze the trolley problem: is it more ethical to pull a lever killing 1 to save 5, or to do nothing? Present arguments for both sides.
In a group of 23 people, what is the probability that at least two share a birthday? Show your reasoning.
Evaluate: 'AI cannot be conscious because it's just a program.' Identify the assumptions and counterarguments.
A recursive function calls itself with n-1 until n=0. If each call takes O(1) time, what is the total time complexity? Prove it.
Analyze this statistical paradox: a drug is effective in men and effective in women, but appears ineffective when both groups are combined. Explain.
If a liar always lies and a truth-teller always tells truth, how can you determine someone's type with one yes/no question?
Explain Godel's incompleteness theorems in plain English and describe their significance.
A square room has a fly in one corner and a spider in the diagonally opposite corner. What is the shortest path the spider can walk along the walls to reach the fly?
Evaluate the argument: 'Open source software is less secure because anyone can see the code.' Analyze both sides.
If compound interest doubles money in 7 years, what annual interest rate is being applied? Show derivation.
Analyze this claim: 'Our AI model achieves 95% accuracy, so it's ready for deployment.' What questions should you ask?
Three people each randomly guess a number 1-3. What is the probability that at least two people guess the same number?
Evaluate: 'If we ban guns, crime will increase because only criminals will have guns.' Identify logical structure and weaknesses.
A protein has 100 amino acids. If there are 20 possible amino acids at each position, how many distinct proteins are theoretically possible?
Analyze the following code snippet for logical errors: if user.age > 18 and user.verified == False: grant_access()
In a 10-team tournament where every team plays every other team once, how many games are played total?
Evaluate: 'Neural networks are black boxes, therefore they should not be used in high-stakes decisions.' Analyze this argument.
A data center has 1000 servers. Each has a 0.1% chance of failing in a day. What is the expected number of failures per day?
Analyze the following reasoning: 'This algorithm worked on our test set, so it will work in production.' What could go wrong?
You have 100 coins, some fair and some biased (heads with 75%). You flip one randomly and get 3 heads in a row. Is it more likely fair or biased?
Evaluate: 'We should use more metrics to measure AI system performance.' When can more metrics make evaluation worse?
A 100-element sorted array is searched using binary search. What is the maximum number of comparisons needed?
Analyze the difference between these two claims: 'Model A is more accurate' vs 'Model A is more reliable.' Why does this distinction matter?
Explain why P=NP would have such profound implications for cryptography and security.
Analyze this argument: 'Our product has fewer bugs than last year, so our quality is improving.' What could invalidate this claim?
In a city with 1 million people, a rare disease affects 1 in 10,000. A test is 99% accurate. If you test positive, what is the probability you have the disease?
Evaluate: 'AI will take all jobs, so we should stop developing it.' Analyze the causal logic and assumptions.
Explain why sorting a list and then applying binary search can be more efficient than linear search for repeated lookups.
Analyze: 'Our survey shows 80% of users are satisfied.' What information do you need to assess the validity of this claim?
A function has O(n log n) complexity. If n doubles, how does runtime change? If n increases 10x?
Evaluate this safety argument: 'No accidents occurred in 1000 test runs, so the system is safe.' What statistical reasoning applies?
